era_context: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor is in its final years, with the empire at the height of its power and cultural achievement. The Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia, now in their eighth year. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean. In Europe, the Renaissance is beginning to emerge in Italy. The Aztec Empire in Mesoamerica is undergoing leadership transitions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas beyond Aztec and Inca heartlands

events.title: Yongle Emperor's reign continues; Ming cultural and political dominance
events.description: In 1422, the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) was in the final years of his reign, which had been marked by aggressive military expansion, cultural achievement, and the construction of the Forbidden City. The Yongle Emperor had moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing and had sponsored massive building projects, including the reconstruction of the Great Wall and the construction of the Forbidden City (completed in 1420). His reign represented the peak of Ming power and prestige. In 1422, the empire was stable and prosperous, though the emperor was aging and succession issues were beginning to emerge.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's reign and his major achievements are well-documented in Ming court records and later historiographic accounts.

events.title: Forbidden City completed; symbol of Ming imperial power
events.description: The Forbidden City, the imperial palace complex in Beijing, was completed in 1420 and was fully operational by 1422. The construction of the Forbidden City was one of the Yongle Emperor's greatest achievements, representing the pinnacle of Ming architectural and engineering skill. The complex served as the residence of the emperor and the seat of imperial government, and its construction required the labor of hundreds of thousands of workers over many years. The Forbidden City symbolized Ming imperial power and legitimacy, and it remains one of the most impressive architectural achievements of the medieval world.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The completion of the Forbidden City is well-documented in Ming records, and the surviving structure provides archaeological confirmation of its construction date.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Continued military campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: In 1422, the Hussite Wars continued in Bohemia, with Jan Žižka leading military campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. The Hussite movement had become a major social and religious force in Bohemia, combining religious reform with social upheaval. Žižka's military innovations, particularly the use of wagon fortifications, made Hussite armies formidable. In 1422, Žižka was consolidating Hussite control over Bohemia and defending against Catholic crusades sponsored by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund. The Hussite Wars represented one of the earliest challenges to papal authority and foreshadowed the later Protestant Reformation.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Hussite Wars in 1422 is documented in contemporary Hussite chronicles and later historiographic accounts.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: In 1422, the Ottoman Empire under Murad II was consolidating its power in Anatolia and expanding into the Balkans. The Ottomans had recently defeated various rival powers and were establishing themselves as the dominant force in the eastern Mediterranean. Murad II's reign saw the development of Ottoman administrative structures and the integration of conquered territories. The empire was on the cusp of becoming a major Mediterranean and European power, though it would not reach its full territorial extent until the reign of Suleiman the Magnificent in the 16th century.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in this period is well-documented in Ottoman chronicles and European sources.

events.title: Portuguese exploration: Prince Henry the Navigator begins sponsoring voyages
events.description: In 1422, Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal was beginning to sponsor voyages of exploration along the West African coast. Henry established a school of navigation at Sagres and sponsored expeditions to explore the African coast, seeking to establish trade routes and find a passage to Asia. These early Portuguese voyages were modest in scale compared to later expeditions, but they marked the beginning of European maritime expansion that would eventually lead to the Age of Discovery. Henry's sponsorship of exploration was motivated by economic, religious, and political interests, and his efforts laid the groundwork for Portuguese dominance of maritime trade in the 15th and 16th centuries.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese prince, b. 1394)

sources.name: Later Portuguese historians (e.g., Gomes Eanes de Zurara)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Prince Henry's first sponsorship of voyages is uncertain. Some sources place the beginning of his exploration program in the 1420s, while others suggest it began later. The year 1422 is assigned by convention, but ±5 years is reasonable uncertainty.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1422 as a turning point in world history is debatable. While the Forbidden City was completed in 1420 and was operational by 1422, some scholars argue that its completion did not represent a major shift in Ming power or prestige, as the empire had already been dominant for decades. The dating of Prince Henry the Navigator's exploration program is uncertain; some sources suggest it began in the 1420s, while others place the beginning of his systematic sponsorship of voyages in the 1430s or 1440s. The Hussite Wars are sometimes portrayed as a major challenge to papal authority, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Hussite movement was ultimately suppressed and that the major religious upheaval came later with the Protestant Reformation of the 16th century. The Ottoman consolidation in 1422 was a gradual process, and the year 1422 does not represent a clear turning point in Ottoman expansion.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1422 is dominated by East Asian and European sources, with rich documentation of political, military, and cultural events. The Ming Dynasty is well-documented in court records and later historiographic accounts, though the perspective is heavily weighted toward elite male actors and state-level politics. The Hussite Wars are documented in contemporary chronicles, but the perspective is often biased toward either the Hussite or Catholic side. Portuguese exploration is documented in royal records and later compilations, though the early voyages are less well-documented than later expeditions. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Pacific Islands, and interior Americas remain virtually absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by elites and may not reflect the experiences of common people or marginalized groups.

graph_edges.note: The Yongle Emperor's reign ended in 1424, two years after 1422.
The completion of the Forbidden City in 1420 was part of the Yongle Emperor's broader program of imperial construction that began after his ascension in 1402.
The Hussite Wars in 1422 were a direct result of Jan Hus's execution in 1415.
Ottoman consolidation in 1422 was a precursor to the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
Prince Henry's sponsorship of exploration in 1422 led to the Portuguese reaching Cape Bojador in 1434.
